agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quotes bounded context. The term 'confirmed' already exists in the Orders bounded context with a precise, established meaning: paid and immutable. The new usage means something fundamentally different: verbally agreed but unpaid and mutable.","bounded_context":"Quotes (proposed change) vs Orders (existing definition)","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes are permitted."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (PROPOSED)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms, but payment has NOT occurred. Implicitly still mutable (terms could change before payment)."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A 'confirmed' order is immutable — no field may be changed after confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Immutability after payment is a data integrity constraint. Violating it corrupts financial records and fulfillment state.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to modify a confirmed order MUST be rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A 'confirmed' order has been paid. Confirmation and payment are inseparable in the Orders context.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The coupling of 'confirmed' to 'paid' is a financial integrity constraint. Decoupling it would allow unpaid orders to enter fulfillment.","testable_assertion":"No order may reach 'confirmed' status without a successful payment record."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The proposed 'confirmed quote' has NOT been paid and represents only verbal agreement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a sales workflow policy — the threshold for what constitutes 'confirmed' intent could be redefined by sales leadership.","testable_assertion":"A confirmed quote MUST NOT have an associated payment record; payment triggers conversion to an order."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A confirmed quote is implicitly mutable — terms may still change before payment.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Whether a verbally-agreed quote can be revised is a business policy, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"The system SHOULD allow modification of a confirmed quote (or require explicit re-confirmation if modified)."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A developer queries 'all confirmed items' across both contexts and gets a mixed set of paid-immutable orders and unpaid-mutable quotes.","expected_behavior":"This MUST NOT happen. The semantic collision means shared queries, dashboards, reports, and APIs will conflate two fundamentally different business states."},{"scenario":"A new team member reads 'confirmed' in the quote code and assumes it means paid and immutable, as established in Orders.","expected_behavior":"They will incorrectly believe the quote cannot be changed and that payment has occurred, leading to bugs or skipped payment collection."},{"scenario":"A customer disputes a charge and support sees 'confirmed' on both the quote and the order, assuming they mean the same thing.","expected_behavior":"Support may incorrectly tell the customer 'you already paid' when only verbal agreement existed, or may fail to escalate a genuine payment issue."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Low direct financial impact of the feature itself, but HIGH risk of indirect financial errors. If 'confirmed' is conflated across contexts, unpaid quotes could be mistakenly treated as paid orders, leading to fulfillment without payment.","operational":"Significant operational risk. Shared reporting, search, and alerting systems that key on 'confirmed' status will produce misleading results. Support and fulfillment teams will lose trust in status meanings.","user":"Sales agents get a useful workflow state. However, confusion between quote confirmation and order confirmation will degrade the experience for support agents, fulfillment staff, and eventually customers.","risk":"HIGH. This is a ubiquitous language collision — the most insidious form of domain drift. It does not break tests or CI. It breaks shared understanding across teams, and the damage compounds silently over time.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Positive — they gain a useful status to track verbal agreements."},{"group":"Fulfillment Team","impact":"Negative risk — 'confirmed' in their world means 'ship it.' A confirmed quote leaking into their view could trigger premature fulfillment."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Negative risk — 'confirmed' means 'paid' in their mental model. A confirmed-but-unpaid quote will cause reconciliation confusion."},{"group":"Support / Customer Service","impact":"Negative risk — ambiguous status meaning leads to incorrect information given to customers."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Negative — cognitive load increases. Every developer must now remember that 'confirmed' means different things in different contexts, with no compiler or type system to enforce the distinction."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Confidence is low because the spec contains a direct contradiction with the established domain language. 'Confirmed' already has a precise, load-bearing definition (paid + immutable). The proposed usage (unpaid + mutable) is semantically opposite on both dimensions. Acceptance criteria cannot be safely defined until the naming collision is resolved.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing definition of 'confirmed order' is clear and established","The proposed definition directly contradicts the existing one on payment and mutability"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the business truly needs this specific term or would accept an alternative","Whether the Quotes and Orders contexts share any infrastructure (APIs, databases, dashboards) that would be affected"],"assumptions":["The existing 'confirmed order' definition is authoritative and in active use","Quotes and Orders are separate bounded contexts but may share reporting or UI surfaces"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is being overloaded with contradictory semantics. In Orders: confirmed = paid + immutable. In the proposed Quotes feature: confirmed = unpaid + mutable. These are not merely different — they are opposite on the two most critical dimensions (payment state and mutability). This is a ubiquitous language violation that will cause cross-context confusion, reporting errors, and eventual business logic bugs.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Fulfillment Team","Finance / Billing","Support / Customer Service","Engineering"],"options":["A: Reject the term 'confirmed' for quotes. Use a distinct term that accurately describes the business state, such as 'verbally_accepted', 'customer_agreed', or 'pending_payment'. This preserves the established meaning of 'confirmed' and eliminates ambiguity.","B: Accept the overload but enforce strict bounded context separation — different database schemas, different APIs, different UI labels, different reporting pipelines. 'Confirmed' means different things in different contexts, and the contexts must NEVER leak into each other.","C: Redefine 'confirmed' globally to mean 'agreed to terms' and introduce a new term like 'paid' or 'finalized' for the current order confirmation state. This is the most disruptive option and requires migrating all existing code, documentation, and team understanding."],"recommendation":"Option A. Rename the quote status to something unambiguous like 'accepted' or 'verbally_accepted'. It is far cheaper to choose a precise name now than to untangle semantic confusion later. The term 'confirmed' is already load-bearing in this domain — overloading it is a debt that compounds with every new team member, integration, and report."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Ubiquitous language collision: 'confirmed' is redefined with contradictory semantics across bounded contexts.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 and BR-2. The established meaning of 'confirmed' (paid + immutable) is undermined by introducing a 'confirmed' state that is unpaid and mutable. While no code is technically broken, the shared language — which is the foundation of domain-driven design — is corrupted."}]}
